title: Currant scones
subtitle: Classic plain scones with dried currants, for cream teas
excerpt: Ten currant scones: a straightforward self-raising flour dough with cold butter, caster sugar, dried currants, and cold milk. Mixed just enough, patted to 3 cm thick, and baked at high heat until risen and golden. Serve warm with clotted cream and jam.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the currant scone chapter documents a plain scone dough with dried currant additions, baked at a high heat for a rapid rise. The instruction to rub in cold butter quickly and to work the dough minimally appears repeatedly throughout the Victorian scone literature. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the raised scone section documents the flour, fat, milk, and dried fruit method as the standard British plain scone. Currant scones are documented continuously in British domestic baking from the Victorian period.

content.text: A good scone is almost entirely about restraint: cold butter, cold milk, minimal mixing, high heat. The dough should come together in under a minute of combining. For a cream tea, serve with clotted cream first and jam on top, or jam first and clotted cream on top if you're Cornish.

attrs.body: Flour the cutter (dip it in flour before each cut) rather than the surface. Too much flour on the board works into the dough on the re-roll and toughens it. The cut surface of a scone needs to be clean to rise straight.
attrs.title: Flour the cutter, not the board

items.name: Whole milk
items.prepNote: cold, add most at once and use the rest to adjust

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Combine the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{caster-sugar}} in a large bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter and rub in quickly with cool fingertips until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs. Stir in the {{dried-currants}}. Add most of the {{whole-milk}} cold milk at once and mix with a round-bladed knife until the dough comes together. Add the remaining milk if needed, the dough should be soft but not sticky.

Turn onto a lightly floured surface. Pat (not roll) to 3 cm thick, patting is gentler than rolling and preserves the open texture. Dip a 6 cm plain round cutter in flour and press straight down without twisting. Place on a lined baking tray. Gather scraps, pat once more, and cut remaining scones. Brush the tops with {{whole-milk}} milk for a
Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until risen and golden at the top. The sides should look set and pale, not dark. Cool on a rack for 5 minutes. Serve warm with clotted cream and strawberry jam.
